text: Welcome to another episode of This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Avik from
Zillow. Hey Avik. Hi Adrian. Thank you for having me. Thanks for coming in. So tell us a little bit
about what Zillow does. Of course. So Zillow is the largest realistic platform in the country.
We get the most amount of realistic traffic on our website and we do everything from selling,
buying, providing rentals and providing home loans. So all things real estate come to Zillow,
right? Zillow.com. So tell us a little bit about the interactions that you have with some of your
customers. Of course. So one of the things that we do is connect our potential buyers to real estate
agents through phone conversations and that's where this architecture comes in in helping our
customers as well as our agents. In here we have a near real-time pipeline that allows us to run
machine learning models on top of these conversations to generate insights. I can't wait.
Let's dive in. Sure. So it all starts by calling AWS Transcribe which converts the phone call speech
to text. And one of the important things in here is we use, we take data security very seriously at
Zillow. So we use the redaction API in AWS Transcribe and that scrubs off all PII contents. Say for
example, name, phone number, email address from these transcripts. So once that is done,
these transcripts are sent over to Firehose and we have a limit of one minute of buffering on this
Firehose and every one minute we are spitting out a file containing these transcripts onto S3.
So one file for one minute. So you've got an async pattern to deal with all that volume and
all that data sitting in S3. Tell us a little bit about what Lambda is doing.
Of course. So this Lambda is to handle the trigger that is generated off of S3 for this
event. And this Lambda then pulls this container information from ECR and creates a job and submits
it to AWS Batch. Right. So you've got your container registry maintaining all of the different models
that you have. Now tell us a little bit about how you're using Batch. Of course. So the Batch
is where our machine learning inferences run. We use transformer-based architectures for our machine
learning models. So pretty state of the art deep learned models. And AWS Batch helps us synchronize
between ECR and ECS and helps us scale up and down depending on our job load. For example,
when we submit a job, this job is put in a job queue over here. And this job queue triggers the scale up
and scale down in ECS. So when there's a lot of load, and we get a lot of traffic, say for example,
in the mornings, our cluster scales up. And at night when there are no conversations happening,
we scale down all the way to zero.
So that's a clever way of using Lambda and Batch together to scale your EC2 container service.
So what sort of savings have you seen there?
So just by choosing this architecture and not going with something, for example,
Cronbase or Kubernetes, or doing individual services for the models, because we run multiple
models in a single container through AWS Batch, we are saving up to 75% of the compute cost.
So once you've done that inference, you've matched some agents, what happens next?
So what happens next is we put these predictions into two different channels,
one of the channels being an SNS topic. So the downstream teams can then subscribe to this
topic. And then in near real time, within five minutes of the call ending,
give these valuable insights. And we also send it off to another firehose,
which buffers it for a longer duration, and then puts it into a data leak,
secure data leak on S3, so that our data scientists can run analytics with Spark and Presto and what have you.
Great. So you're using SNS to give near real time data availability.
Tell me a little bit about what the developer experience is like.
Of course. So we use GitLab pipelines and GitLab based builds. Our applied scientists,
they build their models and they push their Python code over here to GitLab. And the model weights go to S3.
And we take both of these and run in GitLab pipelines, and we push the new image to ECR.
So this image tag would pertain to a particular GitLab commit and a particular model weight version.
So you built a pipeline for your data analysts, and they don't have to ever deal with any of the infrastructure.
That's correct. So if your model is not doing well in production, you get an easy way to roll back or roll forward.
This is a great architecture. Thanks so much for sharing it with us today, Avik.
Thank you so much for having me.
And thank you for joining us. And this is my architecture.
